text: Anastomotic leakage following colorectal surgery is a serious complication. Interoperative colonic andrography with endocyanin green can be used to assess bowel perfusion. The popularity and use of this technique is increasing. However, the interpretation of the temporal fluorescence signal is still not standardized. inter-user variability, especially among inexpert users, has been demonstrated. In this video, I would like to show you some interesting phenomena relating to the dynamic visual analysis of the fluorescent signal.

The intraoperative view shows the abdomen of a male patient with rectal cancer undergoing laparoscopic anterior resection. The imperial mesenteric vein is ligated, and the mesentery is prepared. Under white light, a transection point is marked by an arrow. The screen displays three small boxes on the left and a larger box: the top left shows a white light image, the middle left displays a near-infrared signal in black and white, and the bottom left presents a hybrid image with ICG fluorescence appearing green.

Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected intravenously, causing the surrounding tissue to fluoresce green under near-infrared imaging. The planned transection point fails to show inflow, while more proximal sections of the colon begin to fluoresce, indicating better perfusion. Over six minutes, the green fluorescence migrates distally, complicating the identification of an optimal transection point due to the shifting transition zone.

A second phenomenon may throw the inexpert user off course. This reflects principles of the physics of light. Fluorescence is dependent on an energy source in the form of near-infrared, exciting the ICG molecule. Once excited, photons are emitted and, via specialised scopes, these are detected and displayed. These phenomena are governed by distance-related light decay. such as the inverse square law. Thus, bowel at the periphery of the image may display a dampened signal. This may trick the user to miss the rapid peaks occurring at the periphery and instead be distracted by the central bright image. The surgeon may believe that bowel at the edge of the screen is less well perfused than it really is and be biased by having preselected a transection point at the center of the picture.

In a laparoscopic box simulator, the surgeon uses a zero-degree camera to observe ICG reference cards. The distal card appears less bright in the black and white near-infrared view, indicating lower fluorescence intensity. The proximal small bowel is visible in the top right of the video, while the distal large bowel is in the top left. The devascularized ileocolic territory does not take up ICG, and the dye is seen creeping slowly out of the initial margin.

We again track the fluorescence signal in different regions of the image over time. A body of work supports that the chronology of the flow as opposed to the peak brightness may be key to signal interpretation. In upper gastrointestinal surgery, the time point to reach maximum signal has been thresholded to ninety seconds. However, this landmark is not defined in colorectal surgery. Quantitative fluorescence involves measuring the intensity of the reflected signal and charting it on a time plot. Temporal fluorescence curve analysis in colorectal surgery has been well described in this paper. The milestones of this time fluorescence plot are labeled and of interest to us are the peak intensity marked as Fmax and the time to achieve that zenith as Tmax. We can roughly divide the large hybrid image of our extracorporeal right hemicolectomy into three zones.

The video displays a color-coded map of the surgical field, highlighting different zones: the purple zone indicates the devascularized ileocolic territory near the cecum, which does not fluoresce; the red zone marks the area near the blood supply for the ascending colon and ileum; and the green zone represents the transition area in the center of the image. Graphs show the fluorescence signal intensity and time to peak intensity for each zone, with the purple zone showing lower intensity and slower peak time, while the green zone exhibits sharp intensity changes.

This final graph shows that with brisk perfusion, the maximum intensity at the perfused side may be appearing less bright for non-vascular reasons. One plausible explanation is that the camera is picking up a weaker reflected signal off the bowel as the theoretically better perfused bowel sits at the periphery of the image, proportionally further away from the light source and sensor. The implication of this is that the surgeon may be biased towards the brighter areas which are in the center of the image, thus being distracted from the areas at the periphery of the picture which could be better perfused. These cases illustrate some challenges in the interpretation of the ICG fluorescence signal. This might be the cause for the indeterminate results reported in recent trials and necessitates the development of a standard approach. However, it may be the case that this rapid and dynamic interaction may not be fully appreciated by the human eye and may require computational support in the form of artificial intelligence.
